questions.question: Compare linear search and binary search with respect to their working, complexity, and prerequisites. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Linear search: Sequential check, O(n) time, works on any data (sorted or unsorted).
Binary search: Divide and conquer, O(log n) time, requires sorted data.
Linear is simpler but slower for large datasets. Binary is faster but needs preprocessing (sorting).
Linear uses O(1) space. Binary uses O(1) iterative or O(log n) recursive space.

questions.question: Explain the working of Bubble Sort with an example. What is its time complexity? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps if they are in wrong order.
After each pass, the largest unsorted element reaches its correct position.
Example: Sort [5, 3, 8, 1] showing each pass and swap.
Time: O(n^2) worst/average, O(n) best (already sorted with optimization).

questions.question: Compare Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, and Insertion Sort in terms of working principle, time complexity, space complexity, and stability. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Bubble Sort: Swap adjacent elements, O(n^2) worst, O(n) best, stable, in-place.
Selection Sort: Find minimum and place at beginning, O(n^2) all cases, not stable, in-place.
Insertion Sort: Insert each element in sorted position, O(n^2) worst, O(n) best, stable, in-place.
All three are simple O(n^2) algorithms suitable for small datasets.
Insertion Sort is best for nearly sorted data; Selection Sort makes minimum swaps.

questions.question: Explain the binary search algorithm with an example. What are its advantages and limitations? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Compare target with middle element. If target < middle, search left half; if target > middle, search right half.
Example: Searching for 7 in [2, 4, 5, 7, 9, 11] showing each step.
Advantage: Very efficient O(log n) for large sorted datasets.
Limitation: Requires sorted data; not efficient for small or unsorted datasets.
